title: Dataset Cleaning
lesson:
Dataset cleaning is the unglamorous step of going through your training examples and fixing or throwing out the broken ones before the model ever sees them. It is the difference between feeding a model fresh ingredients and feeding it whatever was at the back of the fridge. A model has no taste buds — it cannot smell that something has gone off. It will swallow every example you give it and faithfully copy the patterns inside, garbage included.

It helps to separate two words that sound the same. Data curation is choosing which examples belong in your dataset and in what mix — the editorial decision. Cleaning is making the examples you keep actually correct and well-formed. Curation picks the guests for the party; cleaning makes sure none of them show up with spinach in their teeth. You usually do both, often in loops.

The kitchen analogy

Imagine you are training a chef by example. Every recipe card you hand over teaches them something. But your stack of cards has problems: three cards are exact duplicates, one is written in French, two have the steps in the wrong order, one says "add 500kg of salt" (a typo), and a few are blank. If you hand this stack over unchecked, the chef learns that duplicates mean "this dish is extra important," that French is a normal thing to switch into mid-meal, and that 500kg of salt is fine. Cleaning is reading every card first and fixing the mess. The chef can only be as good as the cards survive.

What you are actually hunting for

Cleaning is mostly a checklist of specific defects:

  • Duplicates and near-duplicates. The same example copy-pasted, or two examples 95% identical. These quietly over-weight whatever they say — the model sees them as "more true." They also leak between your training and test splits, faking good scores.
  • Wrong answers. An example where the assistant response is simply incorrect. In SFT the model copies it as gospel.
  • Formatting corruption. Broken JSON, leftover HTML tags, mojibake (garbled characters like café turning into cafÃ©), or stray \n\n\n runs.
  • Wrong language or off-topic text. A few French recipe cards in an English dataset.
  • Toxic, unsafe, or personal data (PII). Real phone numbers, emails, slurs — things the model should never learn to repeat. PII means Personally Identifiable Information.
  • Length outliers. Empty responses, or a single 40,000-word monster that wastes your context window and dominates training.

A second analogy: cleaning the water

Think of your raw data as river water and your model as the town that drinks it. You would never pipe river water straight into homes. You run it through filters that catch the obvious junk, then test samples to confirm it is safe. Cleaning works the same way: cheap automatic filters first (dedup, drop empties, strip bad characters), then human spot-checks — eyeballing a random sample of, say, 100 examples to catch what the filters missed.

A small, realistic filter

Most cleaning starts as a few dozen lines of plain Python that walk every row and decide keep-or-drop. Here is the skeleton:

import json, hashlib

seen = set()
kept = []
for line in open("raw.jsonl"):
    row = json.loads(line)
    ans = row["messages"][-1]["content"].strip()
    if len(ans) < 3 or len(ans) > 8000:      # drop empties / monsters
        continue
    h = hashlib.md5(ans.lower().encode()).hexdigest()
    if h in seen:                              # drop exact duplicates
        continue
    seen.add(h)
    kept.append(row)
print(f"kept {len(kept)} rows")

That hash trick catches exact duplicates instantly. Near-duplicates (reworded copies) need fuzzy matching — comparing embeddings (number-lists that capture meaning) and dropping pairs that are too similar. For synthetic data generated by another model, near-dup removal matters even more, because models love repeating themselves.

⚠️ Always clean before you split into train and test sets, and de-duplicate across the split. If a cleaned example lands in both, your evaluation just measures memorisation, not skill.

How much does this matter? In practice, teams routinely find that aggressive cleaning — sometimes throwing away 30–50% of a scraped corpus — produces a better model than keeping everything. Less, but cleaner, almost always wins, because every bad example doesn't just fail to help, it actively teaches the wrong thing. Cleaning is boring. It is also usually the highest-leverage hour you will spend on a fine-tune.
mentalModel: Your model is a chef with no sense of taste who copies every recipe card you hand over — duplicates, typos, blank cards and all. Cleaning is reading the whole stack first and binning the bad cards, because the chef can only ever cook as well as the cards that survive.
mistakes: Skipping the human spot-check. Automatic filters catch broken JSON and empties, but they happily pass confident, fluent, wrong answers. Always eyeball a random sample of ~100 rows by hand — you will be shocked what slips through.

Cleaning after the train/test split, not before. Or de-duping each split separately. Either way, identical examples leak across the boundary and your evaluation scores measure memorisation, not real skill. Clean and dedup across the whole set first.

Only removing exact duplicates. Real datasets are full of near-duplicates — the same answer slightly reworded. They over-weight a few patterns just as badly. Use fuzzy or embedding-based matching, not just string equality.

Forgetting PII and unsafe content. Leaving real names, emails, phone numbers, or toxic text in the data teaches the model to repeat them. Strip or redact PII (Personally Identifiable Information) as a dedicated cleaning pass, not an afterthought.

Over-cleaning until the data is bland. If you delete every messy, informal, or edge-case example, the model never learns to handle real, messy users. Clean for correctness, not for tidiness — keep the diversity, drop the defects.

Cleaning silently with no logging. If you don't count and sample what you dropped, you can't tell whether you removed junk or accidentally deleted a whole category. Log how many rows each filter removed and inspect a few of them.
exercise:
Take (or hand-make) a small JSONL file of 30 chat examples and deliberately salt it with problems: 3 exact duplicates, 2 near-duplicates (same answer, reworded), 2 empty assistant responses, 1 with broken/garbled characters, and 1 containing a fake phone number. Write a Python script that loads every line, then: drops empties and over-long rows, removes exact duplicates with a hash, flags rows containing a phone-number pattern, and prints a report of how many rows each rule removed. Confirm you end with the right count of clean rows.

Stretch goal: Add near-duplicate detection. Compute an embedding for each assistant answer with a small local model, and drop any pair whose similarity is above ~0.95 — then check it caught your 2 reworded copies without nuking genuinely different answers.
solution: Hint: Order your filters cheapest-first: length check, then exact-dup hash (lowercase + strip before hashing so trivial whitespace differences still match), then a regex like r"\d{3}[-. ]?\d{3}[-. ]?\d{4}" to flag phone numbers. Keep a small dict of counters — removed = {"empty": 0, "dup": 0, "pii": 0} — and bump it inside each branch so your final report is just print(removed). From a salted set of 30 with 3 exact dups and 2 empties, expect ~25 rows after the exact passes. For the stretch goal, encode each answer once, compare with cosine similarity, and remember to compare every pair only once (i vs j where j > i) so you don't accidentally delete both copies of a near-dup — keep one, drop the other.
summary: Dataset cleaning fixes or removes broken training examples before the model copies them: duplicates and near-duplicates, wrong answers, corrupted formatting, wrong language, PII, and length outliers. Run cheap automatic filters first, then human spot-checks, and always clean and de-duplicate before splitting train and test. Less but cleaner data reliably beats more but messy data.

keyTerms.def: Fixing or removing broken, wrong, or duplicate training examples before the model is trained on them.
Choosing which examples belong in a dataset and in what mix. Cleaning then makes the chosen examples correct and well-formed.
The same example copied exactly, or two examples that are almost identical. Both quietly over-weight whatever they say.
Detecting and removing duplicate or near-duplicate examples, ideally across the whole dataset before any train/test split.
Personally Identifiable Information — real names, emails, phone numbers and similar. It must be stripped or redacted so the model never learns to repeat it.
Garbled text from a character-encoding mix-up, where something like 'café' shows up as 'cafÃ©'. A common formatting defect to clean.
Manually reading a random sample of examples (e.g. 100) to catch problems automatic filters miss, like fluent-but-wrong answers.
When the same example appears in both training and test sets, so evaluation scores measure memorisation instead of real skill.
An example that is far shorter or longer than the rest — empty responses, or a giant one that dominates training and wastes context.
